articles.summary: Cleaning up after AI rockstar developers Photo by Martijn Baudoin on Unsplash We've all worked with a rockstar developer who joined years ago, full of energy and great ideas about new tech and architectures. Their cutting-edge ideas left everyone else feeling behind and outdated as they rewrote the company's core architecture and introduced new build processes. The hardest tasks were assigned to them, and their code was often incomprehensible, leading to a culture where nobody understood it but no one would admit it. After they left, teams struggled to keep up with their projects, dealing with messy codebases that required weeks to fix and sometimes even years to rewrite.

Instant prompt renders a cached prompt before .zshrc finishes loading, rendering the exit-time number irrelevant in practice.

articles.title: How much do amd64 microarchitecture levels help in Go?
articles.summary: The Go toolchain exposes a v1 through v4 ladder for AMD64 microarchitecture levels, allowing developers to target older instruction sets while still compiling on newer hardware. This means that a realistic "use everything this CPU offers" target today would need at least a v5, and the entire scheme should be replaced by finer-grained feature detection. The Go toolchain provides a way to set the GOAMD64 environment variable to use everything up to and including AVX2, raising questions about how much gain is actually made from compiling at each level.
